statusLine: European fuel security is under strain with 20 of 27 countries falling short of the 90-day oil stock requirement as crude prices surge 34% since February.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel reserves are under significant pressure, with 20 of 27 EU countries currently failing to meet the mandatory 90-day minimum for at least one fuel type. The situation is most acute for jet fuel, where 17 countries fall below the threshold, including major economies like Germany (59.9 days), France (65 days), and Italy (61.5 days). Diesel supplies are also concerning, with 14 countries below the 90-day requirement.

The crisis reflects the impact of Middle East supply disruptions, which have forced several countries to release strategic reserves while driving Brent crude prices from $72.48 per barrel in late February to $97.35 currently—a 34% surge. This price pressure is evident at fuel pumps, with EU average prices reaching €1.812 per litre for petrol and €1.821 for diesel.

While EU averages appear adequate (96.3 days petrol, 88.8 days diesel), this masks severe disparities between member states. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania face critical shortages across multiple fuel types, with some reserves as low as 22.5 days. Conversely, Baltic states like Estonia and Latvia maintain robust stocks exceeding 200 days for petrol, highlighting the uneven distribution of fuel security across the bloc.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU countries fail to meet the 90-day minimum stock requirement for at least one fuel type
Jet fuel situation is most severe, with 17 countries below the mandatory threshold including Germany, France, and Italy
Crude oil prices have surged 34% since late February ($72.48 to $97.35/barrel) due to Middle East supply disruptions
Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, and Romania face critical multi-fuel shortages, with some reserves as low as 22.5 days
Significant regional disparities exist, with Baltic states maintaining strong reserves while Central European countries struggle
